coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots@silica-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor for highly selective detection of Cu2+ and thiram

Duan, Chunying
coredata.dc:description: Thiram is a widely used fungicide and pesticide in agriculture, whose ability to form stable complexes with metal ions increases its environmental persistence and pollution risks, posing potential threats to water bodies and food safety. Therefore, it is urgently needed to develop a sensitive method for the detection of thiram and heavy metals. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor, comprised of blue and yellow carbon dots encapsulated in silica (CDs@SiO2) composites, was developed for the detection of copper ions (Cu2+) and thiram. Upon excitation at 390 nm, the probe exhibited two distinct fluorescent emission peaks at 450 and 580 nm. The addition of Cu2+ induced aggregation of yellow carbon dots, enhancing the emission intensity at 580 nm via aggregation-induced emission enhancement, while the inner filter effect simultaneously reduced the emission intensity at 450 nm. This dual mechanism enabled a sensitive response for Cu2+ detection. A linear correlation between the F580/F450 ratio and Cu2+ concentration was observed in the range of 0.5 to 115 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.144 μM. Furthermore, thiram competes with yellow CDs@SiO2 for Cu2+ binding, enabling ratiometric detection of thiram concentrations between 2 and 80 μM with a LOD of 1.76 μM. The sensor also demonstrates excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability, making it a promising tool for the accurate determination of Cu2+ and thiram in real samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots@silica-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor for highly selective detection of Cu2+ and thiram

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiram is a widely used fungicide and pesticide in agriculture, whose ability to form stable complexes with metal ions increases its environmental persistence and pollution risks, posing potential threats to water bodies and food safety. Therefore, it is urgently needed to develop a sensitive method for the detection of thiram and heavy metals. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor, comprised of blue and yellow carbon dots encapsulated in silica (CDs@SiO2) composites, was developed for the detection of copper ions (Cu2+) and thiram. Upon excitation at 390 nm, the probe exhibited two distinct fluorescent emission peaks at 450 and 580 nm. The addition of Cu2+ induced aggregation of yellow carbon dots, enhancing the emission intensity at 580 nm via aggregation-induced emission enhancement, while the inner filter effect simultaneously reduced the emission intensity at 450 nm. This dual mechanism enabled a sensitive response for Cu2+ detection. A linear correlation between the F580/F450 ratio and Cu2+ concentration was observed in the range of 0.5 to 115 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.144 μM. Furthermore, thiram competes with yellow CDs@SiO2 for Cu2+ binding, enabling ratiometric detection of thiram concentrations between 2 and 80 μM with a LOD of 1.76 μM. The sensor also demonstrates excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability, making it a promising tool for the accurate determination of Cu2+ and thiram in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food can become contaminated by various chemicals during its journey from farm to table, with heavy metal ions and pesticides being the primary contaminants [1]. Copper ion is a common heavy metal ion that is essential for plants and animals. However, elevated levels of Cu2+ pose significant risks to environmental and ecological systems. Moreover, bioaccumulation of Cu2+ through environmental exposure may lead to severe human health complications including cirrhosis, diarrhea, vomiting, and motor and perceptual nerve disorders [2]. Moreover, despite their crucial role in boosting food production, the overuse of pesticides can pose direct or indirect threats to the environment and human health through residues found in the environment and in food [3]. Thiram, a pesticide commonly used as a fungicide or insecticide, is primarily employed to store and protect vegetables and fruits [4]. Nevertheless, thiram has been shown to have deleterious effects on microbial communities and reduce soil fertility [5]. Furthermore, its toxicity to aquatic organisms can cause ecosystem imbalances when it enters water bodies through precipitation [6]. Additionally, thiram is highly toxic to the human body, causing various chronic or acute diseases by irritating the respiratory tract, digestive system, liver and kidneys [7]. Accurate detection of Cu2+ and thiram residues in water and agricultural products is crucial, given their potential severe impacts on human health as outlined above. A range of analytical techniques have been developed for detecting Cu2+ and thiram, including thin-layer chromatography [8,9], electroanalytical methods [10,11], and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [12,13]. Although these techniques achieve impressive levels of selectivity and sensitivity, their high costs, operational complexity, and lengthy analysis times hinder their widespread adoption and practical utility [14]. Fluorescent sensors, with their exceptional sensitivity and rapid response, have overcome the limitations of the above methods and are now widely utilized for detecting Cu2+ and pesticide residues [1,15,16].

In comparison with other fluorescent materials, CDs offer outstanding resistance to photobleaching, high stability, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity, leading to their extensive use as fluorescent sensors [17]. However, single-emission fluorescent sensors are susceptible to interference from variations in the sample matrix, instrument efficiency fluctuations, and non-uniform probe distribution [18]. In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence sensors significantly enhance detection stability and accuracy through their self-calibration capability, which is clearly superior to single-emission-based sensors. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescence sensors have been widely applied in various fields such as environmental monitoring, and food safety testing [19].

In recent years, there has been a substantial growth in research focused on ratiometric fluorescent probes based on CDs. These probes are broadly classified into multi-emission CDs and combinations of single-emission CDs with other fluorescent materials like rare earth ions, quantum dots, organic dyes, and metal nanoclusters. For instance, Peng et al. designed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor, Eu3+/CDs@UiO-66, by post-treating UiO-66 with Eu3+ and CDs, enabling highly sensitive detection of Cu2+ [20]. Lu et al. designed a three-color fluorescent sensor using blue CDs and red/green CdTe quantum dots to detect Cu2+ and thiram through fluorescence quenching of the quantum dots [21]. Yang et al. developed a dual-emission probe (p-CDs@ARS) using boric acid-modified carbon dots and alizarin red for the ratiometric detection of glyphosate [22]. Chen et al. created a ratiometric sensor using N-CQDs and CuNCs, enabling the sensitive detection of thiram by selectively quenching the fluorescence of CuNCs while maintaining the fluorescence of N-CQDs constant [23]. Liu fabricated a ratiometric fluorescent sensor by integrating blue and green CDs in a core–shell framework, achieving selective pyrethroid detection with a satisfactory LOD through molecular imprinting technology [24]. However, existing ratiometric probes frequently rely on multi-component hybrids (e.g., rare-earth ions, quantum dots, or organic dyes). These materials introduce synthetic complexity, high costs, potential toxicity, and long preparation times, which limits their practicality for real-world applications. Therefore, the development of a simple and environmentally friendly ratiometric fluorescent material for the detection of Cu2+ and thiram remains critical.

In this study, a convenient and cost-effective ratiometric fluorescent sensor was developed that eliminates costly components and toxic reagents, consisting of blue and yellow carbon dots encapsulated in silica (CDs@SiO2) for the accurate detection of Cu2+ and thiram (Scheme 1 ). The blue carbon dots (B-CDs) were prepared from citric acid and 2,3-diamino-2-butenedicarbonitrile via a hydrothermal method, while the yellow carbon dots(Y-CDs) were synthesized from sodium tetraborate and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) using the same method. Subsequently, the B-CDs and Y-CDs were each capped with SiO2 to enhance their photostability and biocompatibility. As illustrated in Scheme 1B, the presence of Cu2+ led to fluorescence enhancement of Y-CDs@SiO2 via aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE). To the best of our knowledge, ratiometric fluorescent probes for detecting copper ions via fluorescence enhancement are relatively uncommon. Furthermore, thiram can form complexes with Cu2+ [25] and competing with Y-CDs@SiO2 for Cu2+ binding. This competition allows for the accurate determination of thiram concentrations using a ratiometric detection method.

Through this design, we have successfully developed a fluorescence-enhancement-based nanosensor for the detection of Cu2+ in real water samples and thiram in vegetables, with highly satisfactory outcomes. The practical application of the developed ratio fluorescence sensor was finally verified by monitoring the actual samples with the standard dosages.

Aspartic acid, glutamic acid, alanine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, sodium tetraborate, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Mg(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, Ba(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Ca(NO3)2, AgNO3, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, NaNO3, Cd(Ac)2·2H2O, NaOH, CH3COOH, HCl, and NaCl were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co. 2,3-Diamino-2-butenedicarbonitrile was obtained from Anegi Chemical. Citric acid was supplied by Tianjin Tianli Chemical Co., Ltd. O-phenylenediamine was provided by Tianjin Komeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethyl orthosilicate was sourced from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Ammonia and Na2S were procured from Tianjin Daimao Chemical Reagent Factory. Thiram, metazachlor, and dinotefuran were purchased from Beijing Miradar Technology Co., Ltd. 2,4-D and phosphinothricin were acquired from Shanghai Miradar Biochemistry Science and Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium sulfanilate was obtained from Dalian Kaimei Chemical Engineering Supporting Co., Ltd. Prochloraz was sourced from Suzhou Ketong Biomedical Technology Co., Ltd. Ziram was provided by Beijing Inoachem Science and Technology Co., Ltd. Vitamin C and glucose were purchased from Shanghai Bohr Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. and Tianjin BODI Chemical Co., Ltd., respectively.

The morphology of the CDs was analyzed using a Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscope (JEM F200). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Thermo ESCALAB XI+ spectrometer. The chemical structure of the CDs was determined using a Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. The zeta potential was measured with a Nano-ZS90 Zeta Potential Analyzer. The crystal structure was analyzed with a SmartLab 9KW X-ray diffractometer (XRD). Optical properties were characterized using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS1000 and FLS920) and a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1900).

Blue fluorescent CDs were prepared via hydrothermal method. The experimental procedure is as follows: 2,3-diamino-2-butenedicarbonitrile (1 mmol) and citric acid (1 m mol) were mixed with deionized water (10 mL) and ultrasonicated for 10 min. The resulting solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) were then freeze-dried and stored at room temperature in the dark for subsequent experiments. These products were labeled B-CDs.

The synthesis and treatment of yellow-emitting carbon dots were like those of B-CDs described in section 2.3, with slight modifications: o-phenylenediamine (0.2 g) and sodium tetraborate (0.2 g) were used as the raw materials, and the hydrothermal reaction was carried out in an oven at 180 °C for 10 h. The resulting yellow-emitting CDs were labeled Y-CDs.

8 mg of lyophilized B-CDs or Y-CDs were placed into a round-bottom flask, followed by the addition of 10 mL of deionized water, 1.6 mL of ethyl orthosilicate, and 0.4 mL of concentrated ammonia. The mixture was refluxed at 100 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain CDs@SiO2 composites. The final products were labeled as B-CDs@SiO2 and Y-CDs@SiO2, respectively

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay for Cu2+ and thiram
ce:section.ce:para: Cu2+ Detection: The ratiometric fluorescent probe was prepared by mixing 200 μL of B-CDs@SiO2 solution and 150 μL of Y-CDs@SiO2 solution, labeled as B&Y-CDs@SiO2. Various volumes of Cu2+ (1 mM) and deionized water were added to achieve a total volume up to 2 mL. The mixture was shaken at 25 °C for 90 min. The emission spectra were recorded from 400 to 750 nm under 390 nm excitation. All experiments were performed under the same conditions and repeated three times.

Thiram Detection: To a test tube, 50 μM of Cu2+ and varying volumes of thiram (1 mM) were added, followed by deionized water to a final volume of 1.65 mL. Then, 200 μL of B-CDs@SiO2 solution and 150 μL of Y-CDs@SiO2 solution were added. The mixture was then shaken at 25 °C and 200 rpm for 90 min. Finally, the emission spectra were recorded from 400 to 750 nm under 390 nm excitation. All experiments were conducted under the same conditions and repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference capability of B&Y-CDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescent probe was used to evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference capability for Cu2+ detection. To test selectivity, Cu2+ (50 μM) was replaced with 50 μM of various metal ions (Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Al3+, Na+, and Cd2+) following the same procedure as for Cu2+ detection, and the test was repeated three times. Additionally, to determine the sensor's immunity to interference, 50 μM of each of these metal ions was added simultaneously with 50 μM of Cu2+, and the same procedure was repeated three times.

The selectivity and anti-interference capability of the ratiometric fluorescent probe for thiram detection were also evaluated. To test selectivity, 100 μM of thiram was replaced with 100 μM of interfering substances (ziram, sodium sulfanilate, phosphinothricin, 2,4-D, prochloraz, metazachlor, dinotefuran, vitamin C, glucose, aspartic acid, glutamate, alanine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, and S2−) using the same procedure as for thiram detection and repeated 3 times. In addition, to assess the sensor's immunity to interference, 1000 μM of each of these interfering substances was added at the same time with 100 μM of thiram, and the same procedure was repeated three times.

The utility and feasibility of the B&Y-CDs@SiO2 ratiometric probe were evaluated using real samples, including tap water, rainwater, kohlrabi and cabbage. Rain water and tap water samples were centrifuged to remove large particles, then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Kohlrabi and cabbage were cut into small pieces. A 5.0 g sample of vegetable leaves were mixed with 10 mL of acetone and shaken for 20 min. The mixture was then centrifuged, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The obtained filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent testing. In the detection of Cu2+ and thiram using the B&Y-CDs@SiO2 probe, no pH adjustment was required before testing, owing to the sensor's demonstrated stability across the relevant pH range of the samples.

The surface morphology, particle size distribution, and internal crystal structure of the CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM analysis revealed that B-CDs and Y-CDs were successfully synthesized, exhibiting a nearly spherical shape and good dispersion (Fig. 1 A, C). As depicted in Fig. 1A, B-CDs ranged in size from 1 to 3 nm, with an average size of 2.08 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image inset in Fig. 1A clearly shows the lattice stripes of B-CDs, with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which is close to the (100) plane lattice spacing of graphene [26]. The particle size distribution of Y-CDs ranged from 1 to 6 nm, with an average particle size of 3.22 nm (Fig. 1C). The crystal plane spacing of Y-CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the characteristic (100) diffraction plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [27] (inset in Fig. 1C). Fig. 1B and D demonstrate the successful fabrication of CDs@SiO2, and the increased size compared to the original CDs. The average particle sizes of B-CDs@SiO2 and Y-CDs@SiO2 increased to 21.52 nm and 12.42 nm, respectively (Fig. 1B and D).

The XRD analysis was used to characterize the crystal structures of CDs and CDs@SiO2. As shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. 2 A, B-CDs and Y-CDs exhibit broad peaks at approximately 2θ = 21.0° and 2θ = 22.0°, respectively, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [28]. The XRD patterns of B-CDs@SiO2 and Y-CDs@SiO2 show a broad peak of amorphous silica at 2θ = 23°, consistent with TEM observations where no lattice fringes were detected, confirming the amorphous nature of the synthesized samples [29].

The surface functional groups of CDs and CDs@SiO2 were identified using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2B). The FTIR spectrum of B-CDs (green line) in Fig. 2B shows a peak at 3050 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H bonds [30]. Additional peaks correspond to C=O/C=C stretching vibrations at 1690 cm−1, N-H bending at 1560 cm−1, C-N stretching at 1400 cm−1, and C-O stretching at 1200 cm−1. The presence of newly formed amide bonds is confirmed by the C=O (1690 cm−1) and C-N (1400 cm−1) vibrations [31]. The stretching vibration at 3330 cm−1 indicates –OH and –NH2 groups on the surface of Y-CDs (red line in Fig. 2B), providing potential recognition sites for Cu2+ coordination. The absorption peak at 1430 cm−1 is attributed to the C=C (benzene ring) and C-N stretching vibrations. Peaks at 1330 cm−1 and 994 cm−1 correspond to B-O stretching vibrations [32], while the peaks at 830 cm−1 and 700 cm−1 are assigned to the C-H bending on the aryl ring and N-H bending, respectively. Upon the formation of CDs@SiO2, new absorption peaks appear in the FTIR spectra of B-CDs@SiO2 and Y-CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 2B). Peaks at 1060 cm−1 and 796 cm−1 correspond to Si-O in-plane stretching and Si-C stretching vibrations, respectively [33], indicating the formation of Si-O and Si-C covalent bonds between the surface of CDs and the SiO2 matrix [34]. SiO2, known to contain pores ranging from 2-50 nm [35], can accommodate B-CDs and Y-CDs (with particle sizes below 6 nm) within its pores, surface, and interior via Si-C bonds and hydrogen bonds formed by –NH2 groups [18,36]. The formation of C-O-Si bonds during SiO2 shell creation enhanced the photoluminescence emission, as observed in both B-CDs@SiO2 and Y-CDs@SiO2 [37]. The quantum yield (QY) increased from 55.73 % to 66.37 % for B-CDs@SiO2 and from 10 % to 20.29 % for Y-CDs@SiO2. While the absorption peaks of the remaining groups were weakened due to the formation of the SiO2 shell; Y-CDs@SiO2 (yellow line in Fig. 2B) retained signals for –OH and –NH2.

The elemental composition and electronic states of the CDs were investigated in detail using XPS. The full spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. S2A) displayed three main peaks at 532 eV, 400 eV, and 285 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. Fig. S2B shows peaks of 288.85 eV, 286.1 eV, and 284.8 eV, attributed to C=O, C-N/C-O, and C-C/C=C bonds, respectively. The peaks at 401.28 eV and 399.72 eV in Fig. S2C were assigned to N-H and N-C bonds, respectively, while Fig. S2D shows peaks at 533.01 eV and 531.67 eV corresponding to C-O and C=O bonds. As shown in Fig. S2E, the full-spectrum XPS of Y-CDs exhibited five typical peaks at 192.41 eV, 284.93 eV, 398.90 eV, 531.99 eV, and 1071.48 eV, attributed to B1s, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Na1s, respectively. High-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. S2F) revealed C-C/C=C (284.50 eV), C-N/C-O (285.38 eV), and C=O (288.87 eV). High-resolution N1s spectra ( Fig. S2G) showed peaks for pyridine N (398.78 eV), pyrrole N (399.46 eV), and graphitic N (400.40 eV). High-resolution O1s indicated C=O (531.47 eV), B-O (532.22 eV), C-O (533.43 eV), and Na-O (536.17 eV) (Fig. S2H). High-resolution B1s spectra revealed B-O (192.50 eV) (Fig. S2I). The FTIR and XPS results confirm the co-doping of B and N atoms in Y-CDs and the presence of several functional groups on their surface. The –NH2 and B-O functional groups on the surface of Y-CDs provide specific binding sites for Cu2+. The coordination interaction between Cu2+ and the lone pair electrons of –NH2, along with its interaction with the oxygen atoms in the B-O bond, contributes to the aggregation of Y-CDs (Fig. 5B). Upon aggregation, intramolecular vibrations are restricted and non– radiative transitions are reduced, which in turn triggers AIEE [38].

The optical properties of B-CDs@SiO2 and Y-CDs@SiO2 were analyzed through their fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra. The optimal emission of B-CDs@SiO2 occurred at 450 nm under 365 nm excitation (Fig. S4A). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of B-CDs@SiO2 exhibited a shoulder peak at 320 nm (Fig. 2C), attributed to the n-π* transitions of the C=O bond [39]. Y-CDs@SiO2 exhibited a maximum excitation wavelength of 420 nm, with maximum emission at 580 nm (Fig. S4E), and a UV–vis absorption peak at 425 nm (Fig. 2D). Fig. S3 shows that the excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs@SiO2 align with those of the precursor CDs. The QYs of B-CDs and Y-CDs were determined to be 55.73 % and 10 %, respectively, using quinine sulfate and Rhodamine B as references [40,41].

Fig. S4 indicates that the two synthesized CDs@SiO2 exhibit excellent fluorescence stability. After two hours of UV lamp irradiation, the fluorescence intensities of both B-CDs@SiO2 and Y-CDs@SiO2 remained virtually unchanged (Fig. S4B, F). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs@SiO2 and Y-CDs@SiO2 remained largely unaltered when NaCl concentrations were within the ranges of 0–5.0 mol/L and 0–0.11 mol/L, respectively (Fig. S4C, G), indicating considerable salt resistance. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@SiO2 showed negligible changes within a pH range of 4.0–10.0 (Fig. S4D). However, under extreme acidic or alkaline conditions, the functional groups on the surface of B-CDs@SiO2 become protonated or deprotonated, leading to a slight fluorescence quenching [42,43]. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs@SiO2 remained stable across pH changes (Fig. S4H). Overall, these results demonstrate that the developed composites exhibit superior stability, highlighting their potential as high-performance fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of B&Y-CDs@SiO2 to Cu2+ and thiram
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra were used to investigate the response of B&Y-CDs@SiO2 to varying concentrations of Cu2+ and thiram under optimal conditions. Fig. 3 A shows that the blue fluorescence at 450 nm weakened, while the yellow fluorescence at 580 nm intensified with increasing Cu2+ concentration. A good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F580/F450) and Cu2+ concentration was observed in the range of 0.5 to 115 μM. The linear regression equation, shown in Fig. 3B, is F580/F450 = 0.0408[Cu2+] + 0.1839, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9833.

The LOD was calculated to be 0.14 μM based on 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of 3 blank measurements, and k is the slope of the calibration curve) [44]. The inset in the lower right corner of Fig. 3B shows the gradual color change of the probe from blue to yellow with increasing Cu2+ concentration under UV irradiation. The fluorescence sensing system can be used to detect thiram in the presence of Cu2+. Fig. 3C displays the fluorescence response of the probe to various thiram concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F580/F450) exhibited a strong linear relationship with thiram concentration in the range of 2.0 to 80.0 μM (R2 = 0.9904). The linear regression equation is F580/F450 = −0.0240[thiram] + 2.2274, with a LOD of 1.76 μM. Under UV light, the color of the ratiometric probe gradually changed from yellow to blue as thiram concentration increased (see inset in the lower right corner of Fig. 3D).

Previous studies have demonstrated the use of various probes, including carbon dots, for analyzing hazardous substances such as Cu2+ and thiram (Table 1 and Table S1). Compared to alternative methods, B&Y-CDs@SiO2 uses cost-effective raw materials and does not require doping with precious metal nanoclusters or rare earth elements. The probe also offers several advantages, including a broad detection range, low toxicity, stability, and accuracy. Additionally, the method exhibits satisfactory reproducibility and repeatability, with sensitivity at an acceptable level, making it a promising candidate for a wide range of applications.

In practical applications, sensing systems require high selectivity and strong immunity to interference, especially in complex detection environments. To evaluate the sensor's specificity to Cu2+, a series of metal ions were tested under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, only Cu2+ induced a significant increase in fluorescence intensity ratio (F580/F450), while the other ions had minimal effect. The high selectivity for Cu2+ may stems from the strong affinity between Cu2+ and amino/borate groups on Y-CDs@SiO2. Fig. 4C demonstrates that Cu2+ caused the probe solution to deepen in color and change its fluorescence color from blue to yellow. Furthermore, the system continued to produce accurate results even with the presence of interfering ions (Fig. 4A). The study similarly tested the selectivity and interference effects for thiram. The data in Fig. 4B show that only thiram and ziram (both containing dithiocarbamate structures) were able to restore the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm and 580 nm. The excellent selectivity of the probe is attributed to the specific interaction between Cu2+ and the dithiocarbamate structure [52]. These results suggest that the method is promising for detecting other pesticides with dithiocarbamate structures. When thiram and ziram were added in the presence of Cu2+, the solution lightened in color compared to other interfering substances (Fig. 4D left panel). The right panel of Fig. 4D shows that the competitive effect of thiram and ziram on Cu2+ reduced Y-CDs@SiO2 aggregation, allowing the probe to retain its original blue fluorescence. Additionally, the fluorescence response to thiram remained nearly unchanged even with more than 10-fold excess of interfering substances (Fig. 4B). These experiments reveal that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibits excellent selectivity and anti-interference capability, making it an effective choice for the detection of Cu2+ and thiram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of detection of Cu2+ and thiram
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S5 shows that the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@SiO2 at 450 nm slightly decreased with the addition of Cu2+ (curve 1 and 2), while the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs@SiO2 at 580 nm significantly increased (curve 3 and 4). When Cu2+ was added to the B&Y-CDs@SiO2, fluorescence at 450 nm was markedly quenched, and fluorescence at 580 nm was significantly enhanced (curve 5 and 6). These results indicate that B-CDs@SiO2 alone does not respond to Cu2+. However, in the presence of both Y-CDs@SiO2 and Cu2+, the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 is affected (see curves 1, 2, 5, and 6). When thiram was added to B&Y-CDs@SiO2 in the presence of Cu2+, the yellow fluorescence quenched, and the blue fluorescence recovered (curve 6 and 8). In contrast, when thiram was added to B&Y-CDs@SiO2 alone, the fluorescence intensity of both emission peaks remained unchanged (curve 5 and 7). These findings suggest that the observed changes in fluorescence intensity are closely related to the interaction between thiram and Cu2+ [52].

To investigate the mechanism underlying the enhancement of yellow fluorescence, varying amounts of Cu2+ were added to the Y-CDs@SiO2 solution. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity increased in proportion to the Cu2+ concentration. As illustrated in Fig. S6A, the addition of Cu2+ enhanced the absorbance of Y-CDs@SiO2 at 425 nm, indicating that Cu2+ binds directly to Y-CDs on the surface of SiO2, forming (Y-CDs@SiO2)-Cu2+ complexes [53]. This was accompanied by a color of the Y-CDs@SiO2 solution from light yellow to dark yellow (inset of Fig. S6A), in accordance with the absorption spectrum. This color shift is likely due to the increased n-π* transition efficiency [38]. TEM images in Fig. 1D and Fig. 5B reveal that Cu2+ addition caused aggregation of Y-CDs@SiO2. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of Y-CDs@SiO2 increased from 1.61 ns to 1.98 ns with Cu2+ addition (Fig. 5C), suggesting that the chelation of Cu2+ with Y-CDs@SiO2 suppressed intramolecular vibrations and inhibited non-radiative processes [54]. The zeta potential of Y-CDs@SiO2 also changed significantly, from −8.31 mV to −0.78 mV, after Cu2+ addition (Fig. S6B), further supporting the formation of Cu2+-(Y-CDs@SiO2). These results suggest that the fluorescence enhancement of Y-CDs@SiO2 is due to the strong affinity of Cu2+ for the amino groups on Y-CDs, which leads to the aggregation of Y-CDs@SiO2 and inhibition of non-radiative processes. This fluorescence enhancement can therefore be attributed to Cu2+-induced AIEE. Fig. S7A demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs@SiO2 increased with the proportion of ethanol, further confirming the AIEE mechanism [55].

The fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 in the ratiometric fluorescent probe was quenched upon the addition of Cu2+. The UV–vis absorption spectra of B-CDs@SiO2 did not show any new peaks after Cu2+ addition (Fig. S6C) indicating that Cu2+ and B-CDs@SiO2 did not form non-fluorescent complexes [56]. In contrast, aggregated Y-CDs@SiO2 exhibited an enhanced UV–vis absorption at 425 nm, which largely overlapped with the emission spectrum of B-CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 5D). This suggests that the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs@SiO2 in the probe is due to inner filter effect (IFE) [57]. Fluorescence lifetime measurements provide further insight into the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. S7C and D, the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs@SiO2 was 3.64 ns, which remained unchanged after the addition of Y-CDs@SiO2 and Cu2+. The unaltered fluorescence lifetime indicates that the quenching process does not involve energy transfer, excluding Förster resonance energy transfer [58].

Continued addition of thiram to B&Y-CDs@SiO2 in the presence of Cu2+ resulted in a significant recovery of the fluorescence intensity at both emission peaks (curve 5, 6, and 8 in Fig. S5). The blue line in Fig. S6D indicates the emergence of a new UV–vis absorption peak at 420 nm, suggesting the formation of complexes between thiram and Cu2+. The inset shows that the colorless thiram solution turned yellow upon Cu2+ addition. Research has shown that Cu2+ can facilitate the degradation of thiram, weakening the S-S bond and forming a complex between the dithiocarbamate anion and Cu2+ (Scheme S1) [59,60]. When thiram was added to a solution of Y-CDs@SiO2 containing Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs@SiO2 decreased (Fig. S8), indicating that thiram competes with Y-CDs@SiO2 for Cu2+. This results in reduced aggregation of Y-CDs@SiO2 as thiram concentration increases. This phenomenon is likely due to the greater stability of the thiram-Cu2+ complex compared to the (Y-CDs@SiO2)-Cu2+ complex. However, thiram addition did not fully restore fluorescence intensity, likely because (1) the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the thiram-Cu2+ complex overlaps with the emission spectrum of B-CDs@SiO2 ( Fig. S7B), and (2) a small amount of Cu2+ remains bound to Y-CDs@SiO2.

To further validate the applicability and reliability of the newly developed fluorescent nanosensor, spiking tests were conducted on real water samples (including tap and rain water) and vegetable samples (such as kohlrabi and cabbage) using the standard addition method. Each treated sample was analyzed three times, and the average recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated (Table 2 ). As shown in Table 2, the recoveries for Cu2+ range from 90.1 % to 109.4 %, with RSDs below 7 %. For thiram, the recovery rates are between 99.1 % and 104.6 %, exhibiting.

RSDs lower than 5 %. These findings confirm the fluorescence sensor as a reliable and efficient tool for the detection of Cu2+ and thiram, supporting its potential in food safety monitoring applications.

In summary, a facile and inexpensive ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on blue and yellow carbon dots encapsulated in silica was developed for the detection of Cu2+ and thiram. The all-carbon-based design eliminates reliance on rare-earth elements or metal nanomaterials, ensuring environmental safety. The introduction of Cu2+ activates the distinctive AIEE of Y-CDs@SiO2, leading to a significant enhancement in its fluorescence intensity. Concurrently, the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 is quenched due to IFE. This inverse synergistic response mechanism of dual emission channels has been successfully employed to construct a highly reliable Cu2+ ratio detection model. Under optimal conditions, the newly developed fluorescent nanosensor exhibited outstanding selectivity for Cu2+ and thiram over other metal ions and pesticides. Moreover, the sensor showed a robust concentration-dependent response, achieving remarkably low LODs of 0.144 μM for Cu2+ and 1.76 μM for thiram, respectively.

The fluorescence properties of CDs@SiO2 demonstrate remarkable stability under various conditions. The probe's practical utility was demonstrated by analyzing Cu2+ in water samples and thiram in vegetables, yielding good recoveries with low RSD. Due to its simplicity, ease of use, and reliability, this probe shows great potential for applications in agricultural production, particularly for simultaneous detection of heavy metals and pesticide residues.
